sentence_reviews.text: Sparse lexical retrieval ranks documents from token overlap or learned term weights, typically through inverted indexes and relevance scoring such as BM25.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Reviewed as synthesis across cited source pointers and, where applicable, the canonical atlas graph context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Former ambiguity resolved by narrowing the audit interpretation to synthesis support rather than requiring exact-source wording.

sentence_reviews.text: Stored or retrieved information can be stale, private, contradictory, inaccessible, or incorrectly ranked at the moment it is needed. Its direct dependencies include External retrieval & ranking, Tokens, vocabularies & symbolic units.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Synthesized from cited node sources plus the atlas dependency context in data/atlas.json.
sentence_reviews.notes: Limitation is reviewed as a broad synthesis/tradeoff statement for the family-level node, not as a direct quotation from one source.

sentence_reviews.text: Sparse lexical retrieval stores, selects, or obtains information through Tokens, vocabularies & symbolic units.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: The packet references support tokens_vocab or the node mechanism broadly, but the dependency rationale is graph-derived.
sentence_reviews.notes: Relationship is plausible but should be verified with a dependency-specific source for exact wording.

sentence_reviews.text: In this atlas graph, Sparse lexical retrieval is modeled as depending on External retrieval & ranking, Tokens, vocabularies & symbolic units and feeding downstream nodes such as Hybrid retrieval & reranking, External databases, APIs & live tools.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: data/atlas.json canonical node and dependency graph; generated from node dependencies and downstream consumers.
sentence_reviews.notes: This is an explicit internal atlas-graph positioning claim, not an external-world necessity claim.
